subject: Physics
q: A moving coil galvanometer has resistance <math><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and it indicates full deflection at <math><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> current. A voltmeter is made using this galvanometer and a <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance. The maximum voltage, that can be measured using this voltmeter, will be close to:
question: A moving coil galvanometer has resistance <math><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and it indicates full deflection at <math><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> current. A voltmeter is made using this galvanometer and a <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance. The maximum voltage, that can be measured using this voltmeter, will be close to:
solution:
Vmax=4×10-3×50+4×10-3×5×103
Vmax=20.2 V

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit, the current flowing through the resistance 20 Ω is 0.3 A, while the ammeter reads 0.9 A. The value of R1 is _____Ω.
question: In the given circuit, the current flowing through the resistance 20 Ω is 0.3 A, while the ammeter reads 0.9 A. The value of R1 is _____Ω.
solution:
Given, i1=0.3 A, i1+i2+i3=0.9 A

So, VAB=i1×20 Ω=0.3×20 V=6 V

i2=6 V15Ω=25 A

Now,

i1+i2+i3=910 A

⇒310+25+i3=910

⇒710+i3=910

⇒i3=0.2 A

So, i3×R1=6 V

⇒(0.2)R1=6

⇒R1=60.2=30 Ω

subject: Physics
q: The heat generated in 1 minute between points $A$ and $B$ in the given circuit, when a battery of 9 V with internal resistance of $1 \Omega$ is connected across these points is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
question: The heat generated in 1 minute between points $A$ and $B$ in the given circuit, when a battery of 9 V with internal resistance of $1 \Omega$ is connected across these points is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
solution:
Wheatstone bridge with P = 1Ω, Q = 2Ω, R = 2Ω, S = 4Ω
$\frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{S} = \frac{1}{2}$ → Bridge is balanced, no current through middle 1Ω.
$R_{AB}$: Upper (3Ω) and Lower (6Ω) in parallel = $\frac{3 \times 6}{9} = 2Ω$
Total resistance = 1 + 2 = 3Ω
Current $I = \frac{9}{3} = 3$ A
Power between A and B: $P = I^2 R_{AB} = 9 \times 2 = 18$ W
Heat in 1 min = $18 \times 60 = 1080$ J

subject: Physics
q:
In the figure shown, what is the current (in Ampere) drawn from the battery? You are given:
R1=15 Ω , R2=10 Ω , R3=20 Ω , R4=5 Ω, R5=25 Ω, R6=30 Ω, E=15 V
question:
In the figure shown, what is the current (in Ampere) drawn from the battery? You are given:
R1=15 Ω , R2=10 Ω , R3=20 Ω , R4=5 Ω, R5=25 Ω, R6=30 Ω, E=15 V
solution:
The equivalent resistanceReq of this given network is,

Req=15+253+30=45+25+903=1603

By applying ohm's law, the Current i through the battery is
i=151603=15×3160

∴i=932 A

q: A current of <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes through a copper conductor (resistivity <math><mo>=</mo><mn>1.7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) of radius of cross-section <math><mi> </mi><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> . Find the mobility of the charges if their drift velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> </mi><mn>1.1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> .
question: A current of <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes through a copper conductor (resistivity <math><mo>=</mo><mn>1.7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) of radius of cross-section <math><mi> </mi><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> . Find the mobility of the charges if their drift velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> </mi><mn>1.1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> .
solution:
Mobility μ=VdE
⇒μ=VdE=VdlV=VdliR=VdAliρl
μ=1.1×10-3×π5×10-325×1.7×10-8
=1.0 m2 V-1 s-1

question: A current of 6 A enters one corner P of an equilateral triangle PQR having 3 wires of resistance 2 Ω each and leaves by the corner R. The currents i1 in ampere is

iPQ=26×6 A=2 A.

question: The reading in the ideal voltmeter V shown in the given circuit diagram is:

q: When a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is applied across a wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, it dissipates energy at a rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the wire is cut into two halves and these halves are connected mutually parallel across the same supply, the energy dissipation rate will become:
question: When a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is applied across a wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, it dissipates energy at a rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the wire is cut into two halves and these halves are connected mutually parallel across the same supply, the energy dissipation rate will become:
solution:
For first case:

V2R=W     ...(i)

Now, equivalent resistance of the combination will be R2×R2R2+R2=R4

For second case:

W'=V2R4    ...(ii)

From (i) & (ii), we get
W'=4 W

subject: Physics
q: Two cells of emf 2E and E with internal resistance r1and r2 respectively are connected in series to an external resistor R (see figure). The value of R, at which the potential difference across the terminals of the first cell becomes zero is
question: Two cells of emf 2E and E with internal resistance r1and r2 respectively are connected in series to an external resistor R (see figure). The value of R, at which the potential difference across the terminals of the first cell becomes zero is
solution:
i=3ER+r1+r2

T.P.D.=2E-ir1=0

2E=ir1

⇒2E=3E×r1R+r1+r2

2R+2r1+2r2=3r1

R=r12-r2

subject: Physics
q: A <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mn>W</mn></math> carbon resistor is color coded with green, black, red and silver respectively. The maximum current which can be passed through this resistor is:
question: A <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mn>W</mn></math> carbon resistor is color coded with green, black, red and silver respectively. The maximum current which can be passed through this resistor is:
solution:
Colour band A and B indicate the first two significant figures of resistance in "ohm" , while the C band gives the decimal multiplier i.e., the number of zeros that follows the two significant figures A and B.

The last band D indicates the tolerance in percentage about the indicate value or in another ward it represents the percentage accuracy of the indicated value. 

According to given color-coding, the values of colors are

Green →5, Black →0 Red →102 Silver →10% tolerance 

so, resistance is R =50×102±10%

The maximum current is 

I2R=P

I2=250×102=4×10-4 A

I=2×10-2A=20 mA

subject: Physics
q: In this figure the resistance of the coil of galvanometer G is 2 Ω. The emf of the cell is 4 V. The ratio of potential difference across C1 and C2 is
question: In this figure the resistance of the coil of galvanometer G is 2 Ω. The emf of the cell is 4 V. The ratio of potential difference across C1 and C2 is
solution:
Current flowing through both capacitors will be zero at steady state.

The potential difference across capacitor C1 will be the sum of the potential difference across the resistor 6 Ω and the galvanometer resistance. Mathematically,

VC1=i6Ω+RG   ...1

Similarly, the potential difference across capacitor C2 will be the sum of the potential difference across the resistor 8 Ω and the galvanometer resistance. Mathematically,

VC2=iRG+8Ω   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) to obtain the required ratio.

VC1VC2=i6Ω+RGiRG+8 Ω= 6Ω+2 Ω2 Ω+8 Ω= 45

subject: Physics
q: The resistance of a wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. Its new resistance in ohm, if stretched to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> times of its original length will be :
question: The resistance of a wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. Its new resistance in ohm, if stretched to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> times of its original length will be :
solution:
The resistance of a wire (R) is related to its resistivity ρ, length(l)  and cross-sectional area A as:

R=ρlA

As the length of wire increases, the cross-sectional area decreases. The cross-sectional area in terms of volume (V) and length (l) is

A=Vl

∴ R=ρl2V

Taking the volume of wire constant,

R∝l2

So if R' is the resistance of wire after getting redrawn and R is the initial resistance of wire,

∴ R'R=(5l)2l2=25⇒R'=25×R=125 Ω

subject: Physics
q: A cell E1 of emf 6 V and internal resistance 2Ω is connected with another cell E2 of emf 4 V and internal resistance 8 Ω (as shown in the figure). The potential difference across points X and Y is:
question: A cell E1 of emf 6 V and internal resistance 2Ω is connected with another cell E2 of emf 4 V and internal resistance 8 Ω (as shown in the figure). The potential difference across points X and Y is:
solution:
I=6-410=15 A

 Vx+4+8×15-Vy=0

 Vx-Vy=-5.6⇒Vx-Vy=5.6 V

subject: Physics
q: In a potentiometer arrangement, a cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> gives a balance point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire. This cell is now replaced by another cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The difference in balancing length of potentiometer wire in above conditions will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: In a potentiometer arrangement, a cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> gives a balance point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire. This cell is now replaced by another cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The difference in balancing length of potentiometer wire in above conditions will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
In a potentiometer, null point or balance point is defined as the point where the deflection of the galvanometer is zero. If emf of the unknown battery is ε then,

ε=(Potential Gradient)×(Balancing length l). Therefore,

ε1ε2=l1l2

⇒1.201.80=36l2

⇒l2=18×3=54 cm

Hence, difference in balancing length is l2-l1=54-36=18 cm

subject: Physics
q: In a circuit for finding the resistance of a galvanometer by half deflection method, a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery and a high resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>kΩ</mi></math> are used. The figure of merit of the galvanometer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>μA</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>division</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. In the absence of shunt resistance, the galvanometer produces a deflection of <math><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math> divisions when current flows in the circuit. The value of the shunt resistance that can cause the deflection of <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, is closest to:
question: In a circuit for finding the resistance of a galvanometer by half deflection method, a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery and a high resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>kΩ</mi></math> are used. The figure of merit of the galvanometer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>μA</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>division</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. In the absence of shunt resistance, the galvanometer produces a deflection of <math><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math> divisions when current flows in the circuit. The value of the shunt resistance that can cause the deflection of <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, is closest to:
solution:
We are given a galvanometer connected in the first circuit in the above figure, where E=6 V, R = 11 kΩ and the deflection θ = 9 divisions. The galvanometer has a resistance of G. The current in the circuit,

I=ER+G  ...(1)

Also, since the figure of merit is 60 μA/division

I = 60×9 μA = 540 μA  ...(2)

Substituting equation (2) in (1),

540×10-6 = 611,000+G⇒G = 6540×10-6-11,000⇒G = 111 Ω  ...(3)

In the second circuit, it is given that the deflection is half that of the previous circuit. So, current flowing through the galvanometer half the first circuit. Hence,

I2= ER+GSG+S ×SS+G    ⇒      I2=ESRS+G+GS

⇒S= RG ×I2E-(R+G)I2

Substituting the values from equations (1), (2) and (3),

S= 11 ×103 ×111 ×5402 ×10-66-62=110 Ω

subject: Physics
q: In the circuit shown, a four-wire potentiometer is made of a 400 cm long wire, which extends between A and B . The resistance per unit length of the potentiometer wire is r=0.01 Ω/cm. If an ideal voltmeter is connected as shown with jockey J  at 50 cm from end A , the expected reading of the voltmeter will be:
question: In the circuit shown, a four-wire potentiometer is made of a 400 cm long wire, which extends between A and B . The resistance per unit length of the potentiometer wire is r=0.01 Ω/cm. If an ideal voltmeter is connected as shown with jockey J  at 50 cm from end A , the expected reading of the voltmeter will be:
solution:
From the given question we take, 

Current in the wire, I=VR=1.5+1.50.5+0.5+1+0.01×400=0.5 A

Resistance of 50 cm long wire, =50×0.01=0.5 Ω

Reading =  Current × Resistance
=0.5×0.5
=0.25 V

subject: Physics
q: The given potentiometer has its wire of resistance 10 Ω. When the sliding contact is in the middle of the potentiometer wire, the potential drop across 2 Ω resistor is:
question: The given potentiometer has its wire of resistance 10 Ω. When the sliding contact is in the middle of the potentiometer wire, the potential drop across 2 Ω resistor is:
solution:
20-V05+0-V05+20-V02=0

4+10=2 V05+V02

14=4 V0+5 V010

V0=1409Volt

Potential difference across 2 Ω resistor is 20-V0

That is 20-1409 V

Hence, answer is 409 Volt

q: A d.c. main supply of e.m.f. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is connected across a storage battery of e.m.f. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> through a resistance of <math> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> <mi> Ω</mi> </mrow> </math>. The battery terminals are connected to external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. The minimum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, so that a current passes through the battery to charge it is:
question: A d.c. main supply of e.m.f. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is connected across a storage battery of e.m.f. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> through a resistance of <math> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> <mi> Ω</mi> </mrow> </math>. The battery terminals are connected to external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. The minimum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, so that a current passes through the battery to charge it is:
solution:
Given, 
emf of cell E = 200 V 
Resistance =  1Ω 
D. C. main supply voltage V = 220V
External resistance R = ?

r=E-VVR
1=20220×R
∴  R=11Ω

subject: Physics
q:
In an experiment, the resistance of a material is plotted as a function of temperature (in some range). As shown in the figure, it is a straight line.

One may conclude that
question:
In an experiment, the resistance of a material is plotted as a function of temperature (in some range). As shown in the figure, it is a straight line.

One may conclude that
solution:
From the given graph,
lnR=-m1T2+C
R=e-m1T2+C=e-m1T2eC
Further, it can be written as,
R=R0e-T02T2

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a potentiometer wire of resistance 20 Ω and length 300 cm is connected with resistance box (R.B.) and a standard cell of emf 4 V. For a resistance 'R' of resistance box introduced into the circuit, the null point for a cell of 20 mV is found to be 60 cm. The value of 'R' is _____ Ω.
question: As shown in the figure, a potentiometer wire of resistance 20 Ω and length 300 cm is connected with resistance box (R.B.) and a standard cell of emf 4 V. For a resistance 'R' of resistance box introduced into the circuit, the null point for a cell of 20 mV is found to be 60 cm. The value of 'R' is _____ Ω.
solution:
Given here, l=300 cm, Rw=20 Ω and ε=4 V.

Using the relation, E2=l1l2E1, we have

E=ACABVA-VB

∴20×10-3=60300×4×20R+20⇒5×10-3=4R+20

∴R=780 Ω

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit 'a' is an arbitrary constant. The value of m for which the equivalent circuit resistance is minimum, will be x2. The value of x is _____ .
question: In the given circuit 'a' is an arbitrary constant. The value of m for which the equivalent circuit resistance is minimum, will be x2. The value of x is _____ .
solution:
Left part of the resistors are connected in parallel, hence R1=am3.

Similarly, for the right part of the resistors we can write, R2=a2m.

Therefore, equivalent resistance will be,

R=am3+a2m

Differentiating both sides w.r.t m to observe critical points, we get dRdm=a3+a-12m2=0

⇒a3=a2m2  ⇒m=32

Thus, x=3.

subject: Physics
q: The figure shows three circuits I, II and III which are connected to a 3 V battery. If the powers dissipated by the configurations I, II and III are P1, P2 and P3 respectively, then -
question: The figure shows three circuits I, II and III which are connected to a 3 V battery. If the powers dissipated by the configurations I, II and III are P1, P2 and P3 respectively, then -
solution:
P=V2R
P∝1R
Req for I circuit by balanced Wheatstone bridge
 R1=1 Ω

Req   for circuit II by parallel combination
1R2=12+ 12+11

 R2=12

Req for circuit III by parallel and series combination
R3=1+112+12
R3=2

so,P2>P1>P3.

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> galvanometer is shunted by a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The percentage of the total current which passes through the galvanometer is
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> galvanometer is shunted by a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The percentage of the total current which passes through the galvanometer is
solution:
Let G be resistance of galvanometer and I' be the current passing through the galvanometer produces full scale deflection. If I is the maximum current, and since, G and S are in parallel so, the potential difference between them will be same.

Then,

 I'G=I2S⇒72I'=8I2⇒I2=9I'⇒I=I'+I2=10I'

So the percentage of total current passes through galvanometer is 

I'I×100=I'10I'×100=10%

subject: Physics
q: Drift speed of electrons, when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> current flows in a copper wire of cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>d</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the electron density in copper is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is close to (Take charge of an electron to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>)
question: Drift speed of electrons, when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> current flows in a copper wire of cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>d</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the electron density in copper is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is close to (Take charge of an electron to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>)
solution:
Given,

Current, i=1.5 A,

Cross-section of the wire, A=5×10-6 m2 and

Electron density, n=9×1028 m-3.

As current, i=neAvd

⇒1.5=9×1028×1.6×10-19×5×10-6×vd

⇒vd=0.02 mm s-1.

q: In the circuit shown, the current in the 1 Ω resistor is:
question: In the circuit shown, the current in the 1 Ω resistor is:
solution: R=3 Ω+2 Ω. Let potential at P=0, ∴VA=6 V, VD=−9 V, andVQ=x. Apply K.C.L. at x,  x−6 3 + x−0 1 + x+9 5 =0 . Solving, x=323  V. So, current in 1 Ω⇒i=323−01 from Q to P⇒i=0.13 A from Q to P

question: Determine the charge on the capacitor in the following circuit:
solution:
After a long time capacitor branch will behave like open circuit so circuit will be like,

Equivalent resistance is
R=6+4||2+10 Ω = 9 Ω
and, the current through battery becomes 729=8 A 
From the circuit shown, current through 10 Ω  resistor becomes, 4(10+2+4)×8=2 A 

Potential available for capacitor is = potential drop across 10 Ω

subject: Physics
q: A resistor dissipates 192 J of energy in 1 s when a current of 4 A is passed through it. Now, when the current is doubled, the amount of thermal energy dissipated in 5 s is _____________ J.
question: A resistor dissipates 192 J of energy in 1 s when a current of 4 A is passed through it. Now, when the current is doubled, the amount of thermal energy dissipated in 5 s is _____________ J.
solution:
P=i2Rt
192=4×4×R×1
R=12Ω
P'=i'2Rt'
=8×8×12×5=3840 J

subject: Physics
q: The resistance of a wire is $R$. It is bent at the middle by $180^{\circ}$ and both the ends are twisted together to make a shorter wire. The resistance of the new wire is
question: The resistance of a wire is $R$. It is bent at the middle by $180^{\circ}$ and both the ends are twisted together to make a shorter wire. The resistance of the new wire is
solution:
Resistance of wire $(R)=\rho \frac{l}{A}$ If wire is bent in the middle then
$$
\begin{aligned}
& l^{\prime}=\frac{l}{2}, A^{\prime}=2 \mathrm{~A} \\
& \therefore \text { New resistance, } \mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\rho \frac{l^{\prime}}{\mathrm{A}^{\prime}} \\
& =\frac{\rho \frac{l}{2}}{2 A}=\frac{\rho l}{4 A}=\frac{R}{4} \text {. } \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ shows a full scale deflection for a current of 1 mA. The value of resistance required to convert this galvanometer into an ammeter, showing full scale deflection for a current of 5 mA, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
question: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ shows a full scale deflection for a current of 1 mA. The value of resistance required to convert this galvanometer into an ammeter, showing full scale deflection for a current of 5 mA, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
solution:
To convert a galvanometer to an ammeter, a shunt resistance $S$ is connected in parallel. For full-scale deflection:
Current through galvanometer: $I_g = 1$ mA
Total current: $I = 5$ mA
Current through shunt: $I_s = 5 - 1 = 4$ mA
Since voltage across both is equal: $I_g \cdot G = I_s \cdot S$
$0.001 \times 100 = 0.004 \times S$
$S = \frac{0.1}{0.004} = 25$ Ω

q: A current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μA</mi></math> deflects the coil of a moving coil galvanometer through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The current to cause deflection through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math> radian is
question: A current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μA</mi></math> deflects the coil of a moving coil galvanometer through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The current to cause deflection through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math> radian is
solution:
In a moving coil galvanometer, the current is related to the angle of deflection by the formula:

i∝θ   ...1

From equation (1), it follow sthat
i2i1=θ2θ1⇒i2200 μA=π10π3=310⇒i2=60 μA

subject: Physics
q: A <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistor has certain colour code. If one replaced the red colour by green in the code, the new resistance will be:
question: A <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistor has certain colour code. If one replaced the red colour by green in the code, the new resistance will be:
solution:
Colour band A and B indicate the first two significant figures of resistance in "ohm" , while the C band gives the decimal multiplier i.e., the number of zeros that follows the two significant figures A and B.

The last band D indicates the tolerance in percentage about the indicate value or in another ward it represents the percentage accuracy of the indicated value. 

 

As we know red corresponds to digit 2 and green corresponds to digit  5 , new resistance will be 500 Ω

q: A $5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery with internal resistance $2 \Omega$ and a $2 \mathrm{~V}$ battery with internal resistance $1 \Omega$ are connected to a $10 \Omega$ resistor as shown in the figure. The current in the $10 \Omega$ resistor is
question: A $5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery with internal resistance $2 \Omega$ and a $2 \mathrm{~V}$ battery with internal resistance $1 \Omega$ are connected to a $10 \Omega$ resistor as shown in the figure. The current in the $10 \Omega$ resistor is
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & V_{P_2}-V_{P_1}=\frac{\frac{5}{2}+\frac{0}{10}-\frac{2}{1}}{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{1}} \\ & I=\frac{V_{P_2}-V_{P_1}}{10}=0.03 \text { from } P_2 \rightarrow P_1 \end{aligned} $$
